is [creative](/wiki/Creativity "Creativity") and [systematic](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method") work undertaken to increase the stock of [knowledge](/wiki/Knowledge "Knowledge").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It involves the collection, organization, and [analysis](/wiki/Analysis "Analysis") of [evidence](/wiki/Evidence "Evidence") to increase understanding of a topic, characterized by a particular attentiveness to controlling sources of [bias](/wiki/Bias "Bias") and [error](/wiki/Error "Error"). These activities are characterized by accounting and controlling for biases. A research project may be an expansion of past work in the field. To test the validity of instruments, procedures, or experiments, research may replicate elements of prior projects or the project as a whole.
is a person who conducts research.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

**Research** is [creative](/wiki/Creativity "Creativity") and [systematic](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method") work undertaken to increase the stock of [knowledge](/wiki/Knowledge "Knowledge").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It involves the collection, organization, and [analysis](/wiki/Analysis "Analysis") of [evidence](/wiki/Evidence "Evidence") to increase understanding of a topic, characterized by a particular attentiveness to controlling sources of [bias](/wiki/Bias "Bias") and [error](/wiki/Error "Error"). These activities are characterized by accounting and controlling for biases. A research project may be an expansion of past work in the field. To test the validity of instruments, procedures, or experiments, research may replicate elements of prior projects or the project as a whole.

The primary purposes of [basic research](/wiki/Basic_research "Basic research") (as opposed to [applied research](/wiki/Applied_research "Applied research")) are [documentation](/wiki/Documentation "Documentation"), [discovery](/wiki/Discovery_(observation) "Discovery (observation)"), [interpretation](/wiki/Interpretation_(philosophy) "Interpretation (philosophy)"), and the [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") (R&D) of methods and systems for the advancement of human knowledge. Approaches to research depend on [epistemologies](/wiki/Epistemology "Epistemology"), which vary considerably both within and between humanities and sciences. There are several forms of research: [scientific](/wiki/Science "Science"), [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), [artistic](/wiki/Artistic "Artistic"), economic, [social](/wiki/Social_research "Social research"), business, [marketing](/wiki/Marketing_research "Marketing research"), [practitioner research](/wiki/Practitioner_research "Practitioner research"), life, [technological](/wiki/Technological "Technological"), etc. The scientific study of research practices is known as [meta-research](/wiki/Metascience "Metascience").

A **researcher** is a person who conducts research.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Etymology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Aristotle_Altemps_Inv8575.jpg/250px-Aristotle_Altemps_Inv8575.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aristotle_Altemps_Inv8575.jpg)

[Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle"), (384–322 BC), an [Ancient Greek philosopher](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_philosophy "Ancient Greek philosophy") and pioneer in developing the [scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method")[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The word *research* is derived from the [Middle French](/wiki/French_language "French language") "*recherche*", which means "to go about seeking", the term itself being derived from the [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") term "recerchier", a compound word from "re-" + "cerchier", or "sercher", meaning 'search'.[[4]](#cite_note-Merriam_webster-4) The earliest recorded use of the term was in 1577.[[4]](#cite_note-Merriam_webster-4)

## Definitions

Research, in its simplest terms, is a intentional search for knowledge.

[John W. Creswell](/wiki/John_W._Creswell "John W. Creswell") states that "research is a process of steps used to collect and analyze information to increase our understanding of a topic or issue".[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECreswell2008-5)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The Merriam-Webster Online Dictionary defines research to also include studying already existing knowledge: "studious inquiry or examination; *especially*: investigation or experimentation aimed at the discovery and interpretation of facts, revision of accepted theories or laws in the light of new facts, or practical application of such new or revised theories or laws".[[4]](#cite_note-Merriam_webster-4)

## Forms of research

### Original research

"Original research" redirects here. For the Wikipedia prohibition against user-generated, unpublished research, see [Wikipedia:No original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research").

**Original research**, also called **primary research**, is research that is not exclusively based on a summary, review, or synthesis of earlier publications on the subject of research. This material is of a [primary-source](/wiki/Primary_source "Primary source") character. The purpose of the original research is to produce new [knowledge](/wiki/Knowledge "Knowledge") rather than present the existing knowledge in a new form (e.g., summarized or classified).[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Original research can take various forms, depending on the discipline it pertains to. In experimental work, it typically involves direct or indirect observation of the researched subject(s), e.g., in the laboratory or in the field, documents the [methodology](/wiki/Methodology "Methodology"), results, and conclusions of an experiment or set of experiments, or offers a novel interpretation of previous results. In [analytical](/wiki/Scientific_modelling "Scientific modelling") work, there are typically some new (for example) mathematical results produced or a new way of approaching an existing problem. In some subjects which do not typically carry out experimentation or analysis of this kind, the originality is in the particular way existing understanding is changed or re-interpreted based on the outcome of the work of the [researcher](/wiki/Academic_authorship "Academic authorship").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The degree of originality of the research is among the major criteria for articles to be published in [academic journals](/wiki/Academic_journal "Academic journal") and usually established by means of [peer review](/wiki/Peer_review "Peer review").[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Graduate students](/wiki/Graduate_student "Graduate student") are commonly required to perform original research as part of a [dissertation](/wiki/Dissertation "Dissertation").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[Scientific research](/wiki/Scientific_research "Scientific research") is a systematic way of gathering data and harnessing [curiosity](/wiki/Curiosity "Curiosity").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This research provides [scientific](/wiki/Science "Science") information and theories for the explanation of [the nature](/wiki/Nature_(philosophy) "Nature (philosophy)") and [the properties](/wiki/Property_(philosophy) "Property (philosophy)") of the world. It makes practical applications possible. Scientific research may be funded by public authorities, charitable organizations, and private organizations. Scientific research can be subdivided by [discipline](/wiki/Academic_discipline "Academic discipline").

Generally, research is understood to follow a certain structural [process](/wiki/Process_(science) "Process (science)"). Though the order may vary depending on the subject matter and researcher, the following steps are usually part of most formal research, both basic and applied:

1. [Observations and formation of the topic](/wiki/Scientific_method#Elements_of_the_scientific_method "Scientific method"): Consists of the subject area of one's interest and following that subject area to conduct subject-related research. The subject area should not be randomly chosen since it requires reading a vast amount of literature on the topic to determine the gap in the literature the researcher intends to narrow. A keen interest in the chosen subject area is advisable. The research will have to be justified by linking its importance to already existing knowledge about the topic.
2. [Hypothesis](/wiki/Hypothesis "Hypothesis"): A testable prediction which designates the relationship between two or more variables.
3. [Conceptual definition](/wiki/Conceptual_definition "Conceptual definition"): Description of a concept by relating it to other concepts.
4. [Operational definition](/wiki/Operational_definition "Operational definition"): Details in regards to defining the variables and how they will be measured/assessed in the study.
5. [Gathering of data](/wiki/Data_collection "Data collection"): Consists of identifying a population and selecting samples, gathering information from or about these samples by using specific research instruments. The instruments used for data collection must be valid and reliable.
6. [Analysis of data](/wiki/Data_analysis "Data analysis"): Involves breaking down the individual pieces of data to draw conclusions about it.
7. [Data Interpretation](/wiki/Data_Interpretation "Data Interpretation"): This can be represented through tables, figures, and pictures, and then described in words.
8. [Test, revising of hypothesis](/wiki/Scientific_method#Evaluation_and_improvement "Scientific method")
9. [Conclusion, reiteration if necessary](/wiki/Scientific_method#Elements_of_the_scientific_method "Scientific method")

A common misconception is that a hypothesis will be proven (see, rather, [null hypothesis](/wiki/Null_hypothesis "Null hypothesis")). Generally, a hypothesis is used to make predictions that can be tested by observing the outcome of an experiment. If the outcome is inconsistent with the hypothesis, then the hypothesis is rejected (see [falsifiability](/wiki/Falsifiability "Falsifiability")). However, if the outcome is consistent with the hypothesis, the experiment is said to support the hypothesis. This careful language is used because researchers recognize that alternative hypotheses may also be consistent with the observations. In this sense, a hypothesis can never be proven, but rather only supported by surviving rounds of scientific testing and, eventually, becoming widely thought of as true.

A useful hypothesis allows prediction and within the accuracy of observation of the time, the prediction will be verified. As the accuracy of observation improves with time, the hypothesis may no longer provide an accurate prediction. In this case, a new hypothesis will arise to challenge the old, and to the extent that the new hypothesis makes more accurate predictions than the old, the new will supplant it. Researchers can also use a null hypothesis, which states no relationship or difference between the independent or dependent variables.

### Research in the humanities

Research in the humanities involves different methods such as for example [hermeneutics](/wiki/Hermeneutics "Hermeneutics") and [semiotics](/wiki/Semiotics "Semiotics"). Humanities scholars usually do not search for the ultimate correct answer to a question, but instead, explore the issues and details that surround it. Context is always important, and context can be social, historical, political, cultural, or ethnic. An example of research in the humanities is historical research, which is embodied in [historical method](/wiki/Historical_method "Historical method"). Historians use [primary sources](/wiki/Primary_sources "Primary sources") and other [evidence](/wiki/Evidence "Evidence") to systematically investigate a topic, and then to write histories in the form of accounts of the past. Other studies aim to merely examine the occurrence of behaviours in societies and communities, without particularly looking for reasons or motivations to explain these. These studies may be qualitative or quantitative, and can use a variety of approaches, such as queer theory or feminist theory.[[11]](#cite_note-Roffee_4–22-11)

### Artistic research

**Artistic research**, also seen as 'practice-based research', can take form when creative works are considered both the research and the object of research itself. It is the debatable body of thought which offers an alternative to purely scientific methods in research in its search for knowledge and truth.

The controversial trend of artistic teaching becoming more academics-oriented is leading to artistic research being accepted as the primary mode of enquiry in art as in the case of other disciplines.[[12]](#cite_note-12) One of the characteristics of artistic research is that it must accept [subjectivity](/wiki/Subjectivity "Subjectivity") as opposed to the classical scientific methods. As such, it is similar to the [social sciences](/wiki/Social_science "Social science") in using [qualitative research](/wiki/Qualitative_research "Qualitative research") and [intersubjectivity](/wiki/Intersubjectivity "Intersubjectivity") as tools to apply measurement and critical analysis.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Artistic research has been defined by the [School of Dance and Circus](/wiki/School_of_Dance_and_Circus "School of Dance and Circus") (Dans och Cirkushögskolan, DOCH), Stockholm in the following manner – "Artistic research is to investigate and test with the purpose of gaining knowledge within and for our artistic disciplines. It is based on artistic practices, methods, and criticality. Through presented documentation, the insights gained shall be placed in a context."[[14]](#cite_note-DOCH-14) Artistic research aims to enhance knowledge and understanding with presentation of the arts.[[15]](#cite_note-15) A simpler understanding by [Julian Klein](/w/index.php?title=Julian_Klein&action=edit&redlink=1 "Julian Klein (page does not exist)") defines artistic research as any kind of research employing the artistic mode of perception.[[16]](#cite_note-16) For a survey of the central problematics of today's artistic research, see [Giaco Schiesser](/wiki/Giaco_Schiesser "Giaco Schiesser").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

According to artist [Hakan Topal](/wiki/Hakan_Topal "Hakan Topal"), in artistic research, "perhaps more so than other disciplines, intuition is utilized as a method to identify a wide range of new and unexpected productive modalities".[[18]](#cite_note-18) Most writers, whether of fiction or non-fiction books, also have to do research to support their creative work. This may be factual, historical, or background research. Background research could include, for example, geographical or procedural research.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The [Society for Artistic Research](/wiki/Society_for_Artistic_Research "Society for Artistic Research") (SAR) publishes the triannual *Journal for Artistic Research* (*JAR*),[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) an international, online, [open access](/wiki/Open_access "Open access"), and [peer-reviewed](/wiki/Peer-review "Peer-review") [journal](/wiki/Academic_journal "Academic journal") for the identification, publication, and [dissemination](/wiki/Dissemination "Dissemination") of artistic research and its methodologies, from all arts disciplines and it runs the *Research Catalogue* (RC),[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) a searchable, documentary [database](/wiki/Database "Database") of artistic research, to which anyone can contribute.

Patricia Leavy addresses eight arts-based research (ABR) genres: narrative inquiry, fiction-based research, poetry, music, dance, theatre, film, and visual art.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2016, the [European League of Institutes of the Arts](/wiki/European_League_of_Institutes_of_the_Arts "European League of Institutes of the Arts") launched *The Florence Principles' on the Doctorate in the Arts*.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The Florence Principles relating to the Salzburg Principles and the Salzburg Recommendations of the [European University Association](/wiki/European_University_Association "European University Association") name seven points of attention to specify the Doctorate / PhD in the Arts compared to a scientific doctorate / PhD. The Florence Principles have been endorsed and are supported also by [AEC](/wiki/European_Association_of_Conservatoires "European Association of Conservatoires"), [CILECT](/wiki/CILECT "CILECT"), CUMULUS and [SAR](/wiki/Society_for_Artistic_Research "Society for Artistic Research").

### Historical research

Main article: [Historical method](/wiki/Historical_method "Historical method")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Jebens%2C_Adolf_-_Leopold_von_Ranke_%28detail%29_-_1875.jpg/250px-Jebens%2C_Adolf_-_Leopold_von_Ranke_%28detail%29_-_1875.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jebens,_Adolf_-_Leopold_von_Ranke_(detail)_-_1875.jpg)

[Leopold von Ranke](/wiki/Leopold_von_Ranke "Leopold von Ranke") (1795–1886), a [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") historian and a founder of modern source-based history

The [historical method](/wiki/Historical_method "Historical method") comprises the techniques and guidelines by which historians use historical sources and other evidence to research and then to write history. There are various history guidelines that are commonly used by historians in their work, under the headings of external criticism, internal criticism, and synthesis. This includes [lower criticism](/wiki/Lower_criticism "Lower criticism") and sensual criticism. Though items may vary depending on the subject matter and researcher, the following concepts are part of most formal historical research:[[27]](#cite_note-Garraghan_(1946)-27)

* [Identification](/wiki/Identification_(information) "Identification (information)") of origin date
* [Evidence](/wiki/Evidence "Evidence") of localization
* [Recognition](/wiki/Recognition_(sociology) "Recognition (sociology)") of authorship
* [Analysis](/wiki/Analysis "Analysis") of data
* Identification of [integrity](/wiki/Integrity "Integrity")
* Attribution of [credibility](/wiki/Credibility "Credibility")

## Steps in conducting research

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Research_design_and_evidence.svg/250px-Research_design_and_evidence.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Research_design_and_evidence.svg)

Research design and evidence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Research_cycle.svg/250px-Research_cycle.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Research_cycle.svg)

Research cycle

Research is often conducted using the hourglass model structure of research.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The hourglass model starts with a broad spectrum for research, focusing in on the required information through the method of the project (like the neck of the hourglass), then expands the research in the form of discussion and results. The major steps in conducting research are:[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECreswell20088–9-29)

* Identification of research problem
* [Literature review](/wiki/Literature_review "Literature review")
* Specifying the purpose of research
* Determining specific [research questions](/wiki/Research_question "Research question")
* Specification of a [conceptual framework](/wiki/Conceptual_framework "Conceptual framework"), sometimes including a set of hypotheses[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* Choice of a methodology (for data collection)
* Data collection
* Verifying data
* Analyzing and interpreting the data
* Reporting and evaluating research
* Communicating the research findings and, possibly, recommendations

The steps generally represent the overall process; however, they should be viewed as an ever-changing iterative process rather than a fixed set of steps.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Most research begins with a general statement of the problem, or rather, the purpose for engaging in the study.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The literature review identifies flaws or holes in previous research which provides justification for the study. Often, a [literature review](/wiki/Literature_review "Literature review") is conducted in a given subject area before a [research question](/wiki/Research_question "Research question") is identified. A gap in the current literature, as identified by a researcher, then engenders a research question. The research question may be parallel to the [hypothesis](/wiki/Hypothesis "Hypothesis"). The hypothesis is the supposition to be tested. The researcher(s) collects data to test the hypothesis. The researcher(s) then analyzes and interprets the data via a variety of statistical methods, engaging in what is known as [empirical research](/wiki/Empirical_research "Empirical research"). The results of the data analysis in rejecting or failing to reject the [null hypothesis](/wiki/Null_hypothesis "Null hypothesis") are then reported and evaluated. At the end, the researcher may discuss avenues for [further research](/wiki/Further_research_is_needed "Further research is needed"). However, some researchers advocate for the reverse approach: starting with articulating findings and discussion of them, moving "up" to identification of a research problem that emerges in the findings and literature review. The reverse approach is justified by the transactional nature of the research endeavor where research inquiry, research questions, research method, relevant research literature, and so on are not fully known until the findings have fully emerged and been interpreted.

[Rudolph Rummel](/wiki/Rudolph_Rummel "Rudolph Rummel") says, "... no researcher should accept any one or two tests as definitive. It is only when a range of tests are consistent over many kinds of data, researchers, and methods can one have confidence in the results."[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") in [Meno](/wiki/Meno "Meno") talks about an inherent difficulty, if not a paradox, of doing research that can be paraphrased in the following way, "If you know what you're searching for, why do you search for it?! [i.e., you have already found it] If you don't know what you're searching for, what are you searching for?!"[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Research methods

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/NYC_Public_Library_Research_Room_Jan_2006-1-_3.jpg/250px-NYC_Public_Library_Research_Room_Jan_2006-1-_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_Public_Library_Research_Room_Jan_2006-1-_3.jpg)

The research room at the New York Public Library, an example of [secondary research](/wiki/Secondary_research "Secondary research") in progress

The goal of the research process is to produce new knowledge or deepen understanding of a topic or issue. This process takes three main forms (although, as previously discussed, the boundaries between them may be obscure):

* [Exploratory research](/wiki/Exploratory_research "Exploratory research"), which helps to identify and define a problem or question.
* [Constructive research](/wiki/Constructive_research "Constructive research"), which tests theories and proposes solutions to a problem or question.
* [Empirical research](/wiki/Empirical_research "Empirical research"), which tests the feasibility of a solution using [empirical evidence](/wiki/Empirical_evidence "Empirical evidence").

There are two major types of empirical research design: qualitative research and quantitative research. Researchers choose qualitative or quantitative methods according to the nature of the research topic they want to investigate and the research questions they aim to answer:

**Qualitative research**
[Qualitative research](/wiki/Qualitative_research "Qualitative research") refers to much more subjective non-quantitative, use different methods of collecting data, analyzing data, interpreting data for meanings, definitions, characteristics, symbols metaphors of things. Qualitative research further classified into the following types: *Ethnography:* This research mainly focus on culture of group of people which includes share attributes, language, practices, structure, value, norms and material things, evaluate human lifestyle. Ethno: people, Grapho: to write, this disciple may include ethnic groups, ethno genesis, composition, resettlement and social welfare characteristics. *Phenomenology*: It is very powerful strategy for demonstrating methodology to health professions education as well as best suited for exploring challenging problems in health professions educations.[[35]](#cite_note-35) In addition, [PMP](/wiki/Project_Management_Institute "Project Management Institute") researcher Mandy Sha argued that a [project management](/wiki/Project_management "Project management") approach is necessary to control the scope, schedule, and cost related to [qualitative research](/wiki/Qualitative_research "Qualitative research") design, participant recruitment, data collection, reporting, as well as [stakeholder engagement](/wiki/Stakeholder_management "Stakeholder management").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-:5-37)

**Quantitative research**
[Quantitative research](/wiki/Quantitative_research "Quantitative research") involves systematic empirical investigation of quantitative properties and phenomena and their relationships, by asking a narrow question and collecting numerical data to analyze it utilizing [statistical](/wiki/Descriptive_statistical_technique "Descriptive statistical technique") methods. The quantitative research designs are experimental, correlational, and survey (or descriptive).[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECreswell2008-5) Statistics derived from quantitative research can be used to establish the existence of associative or causal relationships between variables. Quantitative research is linked with the philosophical and theoretical stance of [positivism](/wiki/Positivism "Positivism").

The quantitative data collection methods rely on random sampling and structured data collection instruments that fit diverse experiences into predetermined response categories. These methods produce results that can be summarized, compared, and generalized to larger populations if the data are collected using proper sampling and data collection strategies.[[38]](#cite_note-:2-38) Quantitative research is concerned with testing hypotheses derived from theory or being able to estimate the size of a phenomenon of interest.[[38]](#cite_note-:2-38)

If the research question is about people, participants may be randomly assigned to different treatments (this is the only way that a quantitative study can be considered a true experiment).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] If this is not feasible, the researcher may collect data on participant and situational characteristics to statistically control for their influence on the dependent, or outcome, variable. If the intent is to generalize from the research participants to a larger population, the researcher will employ probability sampling to select participants.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In either qualitative or quantitative research, the researcher(s) may collect primary or secondary data.[[38]](#cite_note-:2-38) Primary data is data collected specifically for the research, such as through interviews or questionnaires. Secondary data is data that already exists, such as census data, which can be re-used for the research. It is good ethical research practice to use secondary data wherever possible.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKara2012102-40)

Mixed-method research, i.e. research that includes qualitative and quantitative elements, using both primary and secondary data, is becoming more common.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKara2012114-41) This method has benefits that using one method alone cannot offer. For example, a researcher may choose to conduct a qualitative study and follow it up with a quantitative study to gain additional insights.[[42]](#cite_note-Creswell_Book-42)

[Big data](/wiki/Big_data "Big data") has brought big impacts on research methods so that now many researchers do not put much effort into data collection; furthermore, methods to analyze easily available huge amounts of data have also been developed.

**Non-empirical research**
Non-empirical ([theoretical](/wiki/Theory "Theory")) research is an approach that involves the development of theory as opposed to using observation and experimentation. As such, non-empirical research seeks solutions to problems using existing knowledge as its source. This, however, does not mean that new ideas and innovations cannot be found within the pool of existing and established knowledge. Non-empirical research is not an absolute alternative to empirical research because they may be used together to strengthen a research approach. Neither one is less effective than the other since they have their particular purpose in science. Typically empirical research produces observations that need to be explained; then theoretical research tries to explain them, and in so doing generates empirically testable hypotheses; these hypotheses are then tested empirically, giving more observations that may need further explanation; and so on. See [Scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method").

A simple example of a non-empirical task is the prototyping of a new drug using a differentiated application of existing knowledge; another is the development of a business process in the form of a flow chart and texts where all the ingredients are from established knowledge. Much of [cosmological research](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology") is theoretical in nature. [Mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") research does not rely on externally available data; rather, it seeks to prove [theorems](/wiki/Theorem "Theorem") about [mathematical objects](/wiki/Mathematical_object "Mathematical object").

## Research ethics

This section is an excerpt from [Research ethics](/wiki/Research_ethics "Research ethics").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Research_ethics&action=edit)]

[Research ethics](/wiki/Research_ethics "Research ethics") is a discipline within the study of [applied ethics](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics"). Its scope ranges from general [scientific integrity](/wiki/Scientific_integrity "Scientific integrity") and [misconduct](/wiki/Scientific_misconduct "Scientific misconduct") to the treatment of human and animal subjects. The [social responsibilities](/wiki/Social_responsibilities "Social responsibilities") of scientists and researchers are not traditionally included and are less well defined.

The discipline is most developed in [medical research](/wiki/Medical_research "Medical research"). Beyond the issues of falsification, fabrication, and plagiarism that arise in every scientific field, [research design](/wiki/Research_design "Research design") in [human subject research](/wiki/Human_subject_research "Human subject research") and [animal testing](/wiki/Animal_testing "Animal testing") are the areas that raise ethical questions most often.

The [list of historic cases](/wiki/List_of_medical_ethics_cases "List of medical ethics cases") includes many large-scale violations and crimes against humanity such as [Nazi human experimentation](/wiki/Nazi_human_experimentation "Nazi human experimentation") and the [Tuskegee syphilis experiment](/wiki/Tuskegee_syphilis_experiment "Tuskegee syphilis experiment") which led to international codes of research ethics. No approach has been universally accepted, but typically cited codes are the 1947 [Nuremberg Code](/wiki/Nuremberg_Code "Nuremberg Code"), the 1964 [Declaration of Helsinki](/wiki/Declaration_of_Helsinki "Declaration of Helsinki"), and the 1978 [Belmont Report](/wiki/Belmont_Report "Belmont Report").

Today, [research ethics committees](/wiki/Research_ethics_committees "Research ethics committees"), such as those of the [US](/wiki/Institutional_review_board "Institutional review board"), [UK](/wiki/National_Research_Ethics_Service "National Research Ethics Service"), and [EU](/wiki/Ethics_committee_(European_Union) "Ethics committee (European Union)"), govern and oversee the responsible conduct of research. One major goal being to reduce [questionable research practices](/wiki/Questionable_research_practices "Questionable research practices").

Research in other fields such as [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences"), [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology"), [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), or [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") may generate ethical concerns.

## Problems in research

See also: [Criticism of science](/wiki/Criticism_of_science "Criticism of science") and [Scientific misconduct](/wiki/Scientific_misconduct "Scientific misconduct")

### Metascience

[Metascience](/wiki/Metascience "Metascience") is the study of research through the use of research methods. Also known as "research on research", it aims to reduce waste and increase the quality of research in all fields. Meta-research concerns itself with the detection of bias, methodological flaws, and other errors and inefficiencies. Among the finding of meta-research is a low rates of [reproducibility](/wiki/Reproducibility "Reproducibility") across a large number of fields.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Replication crisis

See also: [Publish or perish](/wiki/Publish_or_perish "Publish or perish")

The [replication crisis](/wiki/Replication_crisis "Replication crisis"), also known as the reproducibility or replicability crisis, refers to widespread failures to reproduce published scientific results. Because the reproducibility of empirical results is the cornerstone of the [scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method"),[[44]](#cite_note-44) such failures undermine the credibility of theories and challenge substantial parts of scientific knowledge.

### Academic bias

See also: [Politicization of science](/wiki/Politicization_of_science "Politicization of science") and [Sexism in academia](/wiki/Sexism_in_academia "Sexism in academia")

[Academic bias](/wiki/Academic_bias "Academic bias") is the [bias](/wiki/Bias "Bias") or perceived bias in academia shaping research and the [scientific community](/wiki/Scientific_community "Scientific community"). Academic bias can involve [discrimination](/wiki/Discrimination "Discrimination") based on [race](/wiki/Race_(human_categorization) "Race (human categorization)"), [sex](/wiki/Sex "Sex"), [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology") or [protected group](/wiki/Protected_group "Protected group").

### Funding bias

See also: [Author-level metrics § Criticism](/wiki/Author-level_metrics#Criticism "Author-level metrics"), and [Impact factor § Criticism](/wiki/Impact_factor#Criticism "Impact factor")

[Funding bias](/wiki/Funding_bias "Funding bias"), also known as sponsorship bias, funding outcome bias, funding publication bias, and funding effect, is a tendency of a scientific study to support the interests of the study's financial sponsor. This phenomenon is recognized sufficiently that researchers undertake studies to examine bias in past published studies. Funding bias has been associated, in particular, with research into chemical toxicity, tobacco, and pharmaceutical drugs.[[45]](#cite_note-45) It is an instance of [experimenter's bias](/wiki/Experimenter%27s_bias "Experimenter's bias").

### Publication bias

See also: [Conflicts of interest in academic publishing](/wiki/Conflicts_of_interest_in_academic_publishing "Conflicts of interest in academic publishing")

In published academic research, [publication bias](/wiki/Publication_bias "Publication bias") occurs when the outcome of an experiment or research study [biases](/wiki/Bias "Bias") the decision to publish or otherwise distribute it. Publishing only results that show a [significant](/wiki/Statistical_significance "Statistical significance") finding disturbs the balance of findings in favor of positive results.[[46]](#cite_note-Publication_bias_Song2010-46) The study of publication bias is an important topic in [metascience](/wiki/Metascience "Metascience").

### Non-western methods

See also: [Indigenous science](/wiki/Indigenous_science "Indigenous science") and [Neo-colonial science](/wiki/Neo-colonial_science "Neo-colonial science")

In many disciplines, Western methods of conducting research are predominant.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Researchers are overwhelmingly taught Western methods of data collection and study. The increasing participation of [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") as researchers has brought increased attention to the [scientific lacuna](/wiki/Scientific_lacuna "Scientific lacuna") in [culturally sensitive](/wiki/Culturally_sensitive "Culturally sensitive") methods of data collection.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Western methods of data collection may not be the most accurate or relevant for research on non-Western societies. For example, "[Hua Oranga](/w/index.php?title=Hua_Oranga&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hua Oranga (page does not exist)")" was created as a criterion for psychological evaluation in [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") populations, and is based on dimensions of mental health important to the Māori people – "taha wairua (the spiritual dimension), taha hinengaro (the mental dimension), taha tinana (the physical dimension), and taha whanau (the family dimension)".[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Even though Western dominance seems to be prominent in research, some scholars, such as [Simon Marginson](/wiki/Simon_Marginson "Simon Marginson"), argue for "the need [for] a plural university world".[[50]](#cite_note-Marginson-50) Marginson argues that the East Asian Confucian model could take over the Western model.

This could be due to changes in funding for research both in the East and the West. Focused on emphasizing educational achievement, East Asian cultures, mainly in China and South Korea, have encouraged the increase of funding for research expansion.[[50]](#cite_note-Marginson-50) In contrast, in the Western academic world, notably in the United Kingdom as well as in some state governments in the United States, funding cuts for university research have occurred, which some [*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] say may lead to the future decline of Western dominance in research.

### Language

Research is often biased in the languages that are preferred ([linguicism](/wiki/Linguicism "Linguicism")) and the geographic locations where research occurs.
Periphery scholars face the challenges of exclusion and linguicism in research and academic publication. As the great majority of mainstream academic journals are written in English, multilingual periphery scholars often must translate their work to be accepted to elite Western-dominated journals.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Multilingual scholars' influences from their native communicative styles can be assumed to be incompetence instead of difference.[[52]](#cite_note-Canagarajah-52) Patterns of geographic bias also show a relationship with linguicism: countries whose official languages are French or Arabic are far less likely to be the focus of single-country studies than countries with different official languages. Within Africa, English-speaking countries are more represented than other countries.[[53]](#cite_note-Pepinsky-53)

### Generalizability

See also: [External validity](/wiki/External_validity "External validity")

Generalization is the process of more broadly applying the valid results of one study.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Studies with a narrow scope can result in a lack of generalizability, meaning that the results may not be applicable to other populations or regions. In comparative politics, this can result from using a single-country study, rather than a study design that uses data from multiple countries. Despite the issue of generalizability, single-country studies have risen in prevalence since the late 2000s.[[53]](#cite_note-Pepinsky-53)

For comparative politics, Western countries are over-represented in single-country studies, with heavy emphasis on Western Europe, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand. Since 2000, Latin American countries have become more popular in single-country studies. In contrast, countries in [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") are the focus of very few studies.[[53]](#cite_note-Pepinsky-53)

### Publication peer review

See also: [Open peer review](/wiki/Open_peer_review "Open peer review")

[Peer review](/wiki/Peer_review "Peer review") is a form of self-regulation by qualified members of a profession within the relevant field. Peer review methods are employed to maintain standards of quality, improve performance, and provide credibility. In academia, [scholarly peer review](/wiki/Scholarly_peer_review "Scholarly peer review") is often used to determine an academic paper's suitability for publication. Usually, the peer review process involves experts in the same field who are consulted by editors to give a review of the scholarly works produced by a colleague of theirs from an unbiased and impartial point of view, and this is usually done free of charge. The tradition of peer reviews being done for free has however brought many pitfalls which are also indicative of why most peer reviewers decline many invitations to review.[[55]](#cite_note-55) It was observed that publications from periphery countries rarely rise to the same elite status as those of North America and Europe.[[52]](#cite_note-Canagarajah-52)

### Open research

The [open research](/wiki/Open_research "Open research"), [open science](/wiki/Open_science "Open science") and [open access](/wiki/Open_access "Open access") movements assume that all information generally deemed useful should be free and belongs to a "public domain", that of "humanity".[[56]](#cite_note-Christen-56) This idea gained prevalence as a result of Western colonial history and ignores alternative conceptions of knowledge circulation. For instance, most indigenous communities consider that access to certain information proper to the group should be determined by relationships.[[56]](#cite_note-Christen-56) There is alleged to be a double standard in the Western knowledge system. On the one hand, "digital right management" used to restrict access to personal information on social networking platforms is celebrated as a protection of privacy, while simultaneously when similar functions are used by cultural groups (i.e. indigenous communities) this is denounced as "access control" and reprehended as censorship.[[56]](#cite_note-Christen-56)

In several national and private academic systems, the [professionalisation](/wiki/Professionalisation "Professionalisation") of research has resulted in formal job titles.

### In Russia

In present-day Russia, and some other countries of the former [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), the term *researcher* (Russian: Научный сотрудник, *nauchny sotrudnik*) has been used both as a generic term for a person who has been carrying out scientific research, and as a job position within the frameworks of the [Academy of Sciences](/wiki/USSR_Academy_of_Sciences "USSR Academy of Sciences"), universities, and in other research-oriented establishments.

The following ranks are known:

* Junior Researcher (Junior Research Associate)
* Researcher (Research Associate)
* Senior Researcher (Senior Research Associate)
* Leading Researcher (Leading Research Associate)[[57]](#cite_note-57)
* Chief Researcher (Chief Research Associate)

## Publishing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Nature_cover%2C_November_4%2C_1869.jpg/250px-Nature_cover%2C_November_4%2C_1869.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nature_cover,_November_4,_1869.jpg)

The cover of the first issue of *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")*, 4 November 1869

See also: [Academic journal publishing reform](/wiki/Academic_journal_publishing_reform "Academic journal publishing reform")

[Academic publishing](/wiki/Academic_publishing "Academic publishing") is a system that is necessary for academic scholars to [peer review](/wiki/Peer_review "Peer review") the work and make it available for a wider audience. The system varies widely by field and is also always changing, if often slowly. Most academic work is published in journal article or book form. There is also a large body of research that exists in either a thesis or dissertation form. These forms of research can be found in databases explicitly for theses and dissertations. In publishing, STM publishing is an abbreviation for academic publications in science, technology, and medicine.
Most established [academic fields](/wiki/List_of_academic_disciplines "List of academic disciplines") have their own scientific journals and other outlets for publication, though many [academic journals](/wiki/Academic_journal "Academic journal") are somewhat interdisciplinary, and publish work from several distinct fields or subfields. The kinds of publications that are accepted as contributions of knowledge or research vary greatly between fields, from the print to the electronic format. A study suggests that researchers should not give great consideration to findings that are not replicated frequently.[[58]](#cite_note-58) It has also been suggested that all published studies should be subjected to some measure for assessing the validity or reliability of its procedures to prevent the publication of unproven findings.[[59]](#cite_note-59) [Business models](/wiki/Business_model "Business model") are different in the electronic environment. Since about the early 1990s, licensing of electronic resources, particularly journals, has been very common. Presently, a major trend, particularly with respect to scholarly journals, is [open access](/wiki/Open_access_(publishing) "Open access (publishing)").[[60]](#cite_note-60) There are two main forms of open access: open access publishing, in which the articles or the whole journal is freely available from the time of publication, and [self-archiving](/wiki/Self-archiving "Self-archiving"), where the author makes a copy of their own work freely available on the web.

## Research statistics and funding

Main articles: [Funding of science](/wiki/Funding_of_science "Funding of science") and [List of sovereign states by research and development spending](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_research_and_development_spending "List of sovereign states by research and development spending")

Most funding for scientific research comes from three major sources: [corporate](/wiki/Corporation "Corporation") [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") departments; [private foundations](/wiki/Private_foundation "Private foundation"); and government research councils such as the [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health") in the US[[61]](#cite_note-61) and the [Medical Research Council](/wiki/Medical_Research_Council_(UK) "Medical Research Council (UK)") in the UK. These are managed primarily through universities and in some cases through military contractors. Many senior researchers (such as group leaders) spend a significant amount of their time applying for grants for research funds. These grants are necessary not only for researchers to carry out their research but also as a source of merit. The [Social Psychology Network](/wiki/Social_Psychology_Network "Social Psychology Network") provides a comprehensive list of U.S. Government and private foundation funding sources.
